question_text: A wind turbine uses a permanent magnet rotating inside a coil of copper wire to generate electricity. Which TWO changes would increase the voltage produced, and why might one of these changes be impractical for a large turbine?

options.label: A
options.text: Increasing the number of coils and adding a stronger permanent magnet; adding a stronger magnet would be impractical because it would be too expensive and heavy

options.label: B
options.text: Increasing the current and reducing the coil resistance; both changes are equally practical in modern turbines

options.label: C
options.text: Increasing the rotation speed and adding iron filings inside the magnet; adding iron filings would be impractical because it would create unbalanced forces

options.label: D
options.text: Using a permanent magnet instead of an electromagnet and cooling the copper wire; cooling would be impractical because it would damage the turbine

explanation: Voltage in a generator increases with more coils (more loops to cut magnetic field lines) and a stronger magnet (stronger field). However, making the magnet much stronger adds significant weight and cost to a large turbine, making it impractical.
